channel.title: Texts on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Texts on emsenn.net

item.title: Knowledge and Documentation: A Survey of Their Relationship
item.description:
¶The Contested Relationship

If knowledge is what a mind possesses when it knows, and a document is an object that records what is known, then documentation is derivative: first one knows, then one writes it down. The writing is a trace, a copy, a shadow of the prior mental achievement. This picture is intuitive, widespread, and contested by nearly every serious tradition that has examined the two concepts together.

item.title: Knowledge and Documentation
item.description:
¶The problem

Knowledge systems face a persistent confusion: they treat documentation as downstream of knowing. First you know something, then you write it down. The record is a copy of the knowledge, a shadow of the real thing.

This gets the relationship backwards. In any institutional context — a legal system, a library, a software repository — the record does not describe a fact. It makes the fact real. A court’s written judgment does not report that someone is guilty; it constitutes the guilt. A committed spec file does not describe that a term exists in the system; the commit is what brings the term into existence.
